If fifteenth-century Italian Neoplatonism trains the eroticism in the 
original Hypnerotomachia, Dallington receives this scheme and massages 
it, not always gently, in the direction of a Puritan Neoplatonism. For ex- 
ample, the "fearefull and horrible Dragon" that chases him into the dark 
labyrinth of the pyramid attracts...

... component, the present work has used Plato (especially 
the Timaeus) greatly, in tandem with Neoplatonic authors and philosophers (Macrobius, Boethius, 
Plotinus, for instance). This is in tandem with Pythagoras, and Aristotle’s Metaphysics, not an 
uncommon assimilation within Quattrocento Neoplatonism (the use of Neoplatonism here 
referring to the humanist revival of interest in Platonic and middle Platonic concepts and ideas).  
Secondly, with regard the iconographic content, I have used Edgar Wind as a means to 
approach the iconographic interpretation of the many reliefs,...

... the 
queen and averaging receipt of six dedicated books per annum.24 Essex 
liked to be linked with Sidney and the cultured, though militant, Protes- 
tant "vertues" his memory encapsulated, and Dallington is not unique 
in making this connection-' In addition to the general puritanizing of 
the Neoplatonism of Colonna's story, Dallington adds other hints of 
familiarity to personalize his text for Essex.26 For example, in a book 
that relentlessly compares its own architectural monuments and plot- 
events to lists of classical prototypes, Dallington inserts local analogies. 
He likens...

... civilizes Cupid,
thus giving visual form to one of the lasting metaphors of lyrical
poetry: “the school of love.” Much as the moralizing,
“Neoplatonic” doctrine of the two Venuses — heavenly and
earthly — first appeared in the verses of the poets almost a
century before the rise of Renaissance Neoplatonism, so too
did the scuola d’amore first appear as metaphor amid the verses
of the poets of love, where love’s civilizing effects were
described figuratively, couched in the language of education
and amelioration.25 This positive understanding of Eros has
deep roots in Western poetry....
